Subject: important information from National City corporate customer service! (message id: TQ638711825I)
David Johnston just quit. Now what?
Body: First Up Plus: Anthony Bass is out of a job, too. For different reasons. ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ NEXT UP By Ben Cohen Good evening. If this is your first time getting Next Up, the new edition of the Star’s evening newsletter, welcome! Here’s the latest heading into the weekend. JUST IN Sean Kilpatrick/CP Foreign interference Johnston quits after weeks of controversy David Johnston is stepping down as the federal government’s special rapporteur on foreign interference. He rejected a public inquiry, stating that it would force the release of sensitive documents that should remain sealed. That decision was not met kindly by Conservatives. Find out what could happen next in this developing story. Althia Raj’s take: David Johnston’s most serious wounds are self-inflicted. The Star’s take: Johnston was given a mission to tackle foreign interference. Instead, he’s become a problem. Cole Burston/Getty Images Baseball The Blue Jays are cutting Anthony Bass Relief pitcher Anthony Bass posted a video calling support for the LGBTQ community “demonic” and “satanic.” He tried to make amends, then doubled down on his beliefs. Before Friday’s opener of a Pride weekend series at the Rogers Centre (where he was slated to catch the ceremonial opening pitch) the Jays sent him packing. Here’s the latest on Bass striking out. Getty Images Business Canada lost 17,000 jobs in May. What does it mean for interest rates? Statistics Canada announced the first net job loss in nine months, raising the unemployment rate to 5.2 per cent. A drop in youth employment was the biggest factor. Find out how the Bank of Canada might respond. Noganosh family photo GTA Toronto man guilty in beating death of two-year-old Wynonna Noganosh A Toronto jury found a 30-year-old man guilty of manslaughter in the beating death of two-year-old Wynonna (Winnie) Noganosh, rejecting defence’s claim that the girl died in an accident, which the Crown attorney said “defied logic.” The more serious charge of second-degree murder was rejected. Read why and what will happen next in Betsy Powell’s report. Steve Russell/Toronto Star Technology AI security is coming to Pearson this summer Toronto’s Pearson Airport will be the first in the world to use HEXWAVE, an artificial intelligence system designed to detect weapons and other “concealed threats.” Here’s how it works. Nicholas Keung’s feature about one man’s ordeal while detained overnight (unnecessarily) at Pearson is worth revisiting. Read it here. WATCH FOR Starting Saturday, the Star will publish extended profiles of seven leading candidates to become Toronto’s mayor. The Tony Awards, recognizing the best of the theatre world, start Sunday at 8 p.m. Check thestar.com for coverage before, during and after. And read our interview with Toronto nominee David West Read. Major changes are coming to TTC bus and streetcar service due to construction. Full details are here. Corey Conners of Listowel, Ont. is just one shot off the lead heading into the weekend at the Canadian Open in Toronto. Is the Toronto condo market in trouble? Sign up here for the Star’s morning newsletter, First Up, and be one of the first to read it when it lands this weekend. Thanks for reading. You can reach me and the Next Up team at nextup@thestar.ca. I’ll be away next week, but Jordan Tamblyn will see you in your inbox starting Monday. BEST OFFER OF THE YEAR Get unlimited access to the Star: $3.33/month for 12 months Get This Offer If you're not enjoying these emails, please tell us how we can make them better by emailing newsletterfeedback@thestar.ca. Or, if you'd prefer, you can unsubscribe from this newsletter by clicking the first link below. Subject: Your Attention Is Requested – Ledger Account Notice
Body:
Security Alert: Ledger User Data Update

Greetings, Ledger User

We discovered illegal access to one of our databases with a small amount of customer data on November 22, 2025. In the interest of openness, we are alerting all clients even though neither wallet access nor financial information were compromised.
             

What was potentially exposed?
Phone numbers and email addresses are examples of contact information. Your wallet money and recovery phrase are secure. But just to be safe, we advise you to use our secure portal to double-check and secure your recovery phrase.
             

Recommended actions:

  • Click the button below to access the secure verification page.
  • Verify and protect your recovery phrase in the encrypted setting.
  • Implement the suggested security precautions.


Verify My Account

For assistance, please visit our Support Center.

Ledger, 27 Rue des Rosiers, 75004 Paris, France

Subject: Jeffrey Epstein reportedly tried to blackmail Bill Gates over affair
Body: Suspended prosecutor warns about "dictator" DeSantis. ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ ͏ ‌ By Marita Vlachou Monday, May 22 JEFFREY EPSTEIN TRIED TO BLACKMAIL BILL GATES OVER EXTRAMARITAL AFFAIR: WSJ Jeffrey Epstein had tried to blackmail Microsoft co-founder Bill Gates after discovering the billionaire had an affair with a Russian bridge player years earlier, according to The Wall Street Journal. The now-dead sex trafficker allegedly emailed Gates the veiled threat after failing to convince the billionaire to participate in a charity fund he tried to start. [HuffPost]RON DESANTIS IS ABOUT TO RUN FOR PRESIDENT. ANDREW WARREN HAS A WARNING. Andrew Warren, the Democratic prosecutor for a Florida county who was suspended by Gov. Ron DeSantis (R), has described the move as chilling for democracy. "No one else should have to adjust their behavior to the whims of the dictator," he told HuffPost's Liz Skalka. Warren also reflected on the possibility of DeSantis becoming president: "If you don’t say what he wants you to say, then you are at risk." [HuffPost]BIDEN TO MEET MCCARTHY OVER DEBT LIMIT President Joe Biden and House Speaker Kevin McCarthy spoke by phone Sunday while the president was returning home on Air Force One after the Group of Seven summit in Japan. Negotiators are trying to strike a deal to raise the country's debt limit. Biden and McCarthy are set to meet today at the White House. [AP]JORDAN NEELY'S KILLER ON CHOKEHOLD DEATH: IT 'HAD NOTHING TO DO WITH RACE' Daniel Penny — a Marine veteran who put Jordan Neely, a 30-year-old homeless Black man, in a fatal chokehold — has said the incident "had nothing to do with race." "I judge a person based on their character. I’m not a white supremacist," he said. [HuffPost] People on TikTok are using iodine to target pimples. But does it work? Here's exactly what happens when you remove fillers from your face. Does one bad apple really spoil the bunch? Here’s what to do with rotten, moldy food. How to actually answer the "where do you see yourself in five years" question. I used ChatGPT to write bedtime stories for my kid. It's not what I expected. Get the latest coronavirus updates on our dedicated page CONSERVATIVES WIN BIG IN GREEK ELECTION A wiretapping scandal and anger over a rail disaster wasn't enough to keep the party of Greek Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis from leading in elections. Mitsotakis, whose conservative party scored a landslide victory, but without the seats in Parliament to win outright, indicated he will seek a second election. [AP]WARNER BROS. DISCOVERY CEO BOOED OVER COMMENCEMENT SPEECH: 'PAY YOUR WRITERS' Scores of Boston University students on Sunday turned their backs on their commencement speaker David Zaslav, the head of one of Hollywood’s biggest studios. Some shouted “pay your writers” in a stadium where protesters supporting the Hollywood writers’ strike picketed outside. [AP]GOP SENATOR THINKS TRUMP CAN'T WIN A PRESIDENTIAL ELECTION Louisiana Sen. Bill Cassidy became the latest Republican to back out of supporting Donald Trump, adding that he does not think the former president can win a general election in 2024. [HuffPost]STAMPEDE AT SOCCER STADIUM IN EL SALVADOR LEAVES 12 DEAD At least 12 people were killed and 100 more injured during a stampede at a soccer stadium in El Salvador Saturday. Authorities said fans attending the quarterfinals match between clubs Alianza and FAS at Monumental Stadium in Cuscatlán became angry after not being allowed to enter the stadium and knocked down an entrance gate. [HuffPost]NAACP WARNS AGAINST TRAVEL TO FLORIDA OVER DESANTIS' 'OPENLY HOSTILE' POLICIES The NAACP’s Board of Directors has issued a travel warning about Florida that accuses the state, and pointedly DeSantis, of being "openly hostile toward African Americans, people of color and LGBTQ+ individuals." [HuffPost]IF TRUMP 'FINISHED' HIS WALL, HOW ARE SO MANY MIGRANTS STILL CROSSING? Trump has continued lying about delivering on his 2016 campaign pledge to build a wall along the southern border. But despite his easily proven failure, most of his GOP rivals — all of whom say the United States must get tougher on border security — thus far do not seem inclined to point out Trump’s record on the matter. [HuffPost] What number comes next? The Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences knows. The unexpected force that may make us get less sleep. How countries are throwing away their best chance to prevent the next pandemic. The case for shame. The next show you watch could be filmed in Mexico City. An Uvalde victim’s family feels betrayed by officials withholding details from that day. Ex-RNC chair ridicules Jim Jordan with cold "reality" of weaponization claims. Chuck Todd rips House Republican for defending Trump’s 180 on debt ceiling talks. Taylor Swift fans wince after she says she's "never been this happy." Dad killed, two teens wounded after grenade explodes in Indiana home. GOP strategist flips Chris Christie's Trump vow back at him. Tuberville draws fire for holding hundreds of military promotions "hostage" over abortion. Former Florida GOP rep. considers drastic move because of DeSantis. I was adopted from China. People ask if I feel "lucky" — and my answer isn't what they expect. Golf club pro makes wild hole-in-one during storybook week at PGA Championship. 23 of the best engagement photos you haven't seen (but should). Subject: Hodl Hodl Desktop — A New Standard in Non-Custodial Bitcoin Trading
Body:
Dear Community,

We’re pleased to announce the official launch of the Hodl Hodl Desktop, a dedicated application designed to give you a more secure, private, and efficient way to trade Bitcoin peer-to-peer.

This new desktop environment enhances everything Hodl Hodl stands for—non-custodial architecture, multisig escrow, and global P2P access—while providing a more powerful interface for serious Bitcoin users.

What’s New

• Secure Local Environment
The desktop app eliminates browser-based vulnerabilities and delivers a safer space for managing escrow activity from start to finish.

• Integrated Multisig Tools
Your keys remain on your device. The application supports PSBT flows and desktop wallet integrations for seamless, fully non-custodial operation.

• Streamlined Trade Management
Monitor live trades, escrow status, counterpart reputations, and payment confirmations from a clean, unified dashboard.

• Privacy by Design
All trade-related metadata is encrypted and stored locally. Nothing is pushed to third-party cloud services.

The Hodl Hodl Desktop is ideal for high-volume traders, merchants, OTC participants, and privacy-focused users who rely on multisig for secure settlement. It also delivers a better experience for those trading in markets with limited access to traditional exchanges.

For Lightning users, support for time-locked contracts makes L-Hodl even easier to work with.

The Hodl Hodl Desktop is now available for download at: https://download.hodldesktop.com/.
We believe this release sets a new benchmark for non-custodial Bitcoin trading and strengthens your ability to operate independently and securely.

Thank you for being part of the Hodl Hodl community.

The Hodl Hodl Team

Subject: Call for Paper: ICATES & ICABEH 2026 - April 24-26, Izmir/Turkiye
Body:
Dear Colleague,

We are delighted to invite you to the 3rd International Conference on Advances in Technology, Education and Science (ICATES) and the 2nd International Conference on Advances in Basic Sciences, Engineering and Health (ICABEH) organized by ARSTE.

The conference will take place at Best Western İzmir Hotel on April 24-26, 2026 in Izmir, Türkiye. The conference will be held both in-person and virtually via Zoom.

After peer-review, accepted papers will be published in a proceedings book with ISBN. Proceedings will be submitted to databases such as Scopus, Web of Science, and ERIC for indexing. Journal and book publications are free for conference participants.

Registration deadlines:
- Regular registration ends February 15, 2026
- Final registration ends March 15, 2026

The venue is centrally located in İzmir with access to historical and cultural sites including Ephesus, the House of the Virgin Mary, Çeşme, and Alaçatı.

Supported journals include:
- International Journal of Academic Studies in Technology and Education (IJASTE)
- International Journal of Academic Studies in Science and Education (IJASSE)

For more information or to register, visit the official websites.

Contact via WhatsApp or Telegram: +905069167881

Copyright © 2026

Subject: Teachers Federal Credit Union - Visa Card Fraud Control Alert
Body: Dear TFCU member, TFCU has been notified by Visa that some members' Visa Card or Check (Debit) card information may have been compromised as a result of a security breach that recently occurred involving unauthorized access into a third partyprocessor's data system. This breach is not associated with TFCU's computer systems. TFCU requires the customer to provide up-to-date and accurate information, including but not limited to your real name,valid U.S. mailing address and residential address (if different), a Tax Identification Number or a Social Security Number,date of birth, and telephone number. Unfortunately, we have had to temporarily delay your access due to missing account information. A temporary block has been placed on your account until we receive this information. Sign On to Online Banking and remove this temporary block placed on your account. If your card number have been compromisedyou will be notify by phone and/or e-mail. Please note that failure to reply within 30 days will result in permanent cancellation of your account with TFCU. Please do not reply to this email address. Confidentiality Notice!This electronic transmission and any attached documents or otherwritings are confidential and are for the sole use of the intendedrecipient (s) identified above. This message may contain informationthat is privileged, confidential or otherwise protected fromdisclosure under applicable law. If the receiver of thisinformation is not the intended recipient, or the employee, oragent responsible for delivering the information to the intendedrecipient, you are hereby notified that any use, reading,dissemination, distribution, copying or storage of this informationis strictly prohibited. If you have received this information inerror, please notify the sender by return email and delete theelectronic transmission, including all attachments from yoursystem.

Subject: The debt ceiling deal's uncertain future
Body: McCarthy and Biden's deal now goes to Congress. Advertisement eNewspaper | Crosswords | Horoscopes Read in browser Read in browser The Daily Briefing YOUR MORNING NEWS ROUNDUP Mon May 29 2023 Nicole Fallert | Newsletter Writer The debt ceiling deal's uncertain future McCarthy and Biden's deal now goes to Congress. Advertisement House Speaker Kevin McCarthy is eying a vote Wednesday on a debt ceiling deal in the House amid pushback from Republicans and Democrats. Also in the news: Turkey's Recep Tayyip Erdogan has won reelection to solidify another decade of rule. Texas Gov. Greg Abbott has remained silent after state Attorney General Paxton was impeached. 🙋🏼‍♀️ I'm Nicole Fallert, Daily Briefing author. Today is Memorial Day, a day to remember American heroes lost at war. It may be a day off, but there's still news to know. Here are Monday's headlines. What's in Biden and McCarthy's debt ceiling deal? In a last-minute deal to raise the debt ceiling, the White House was able to stave off the sweeping budget cuts that House Republicans pushed in their own legislation while keeping intact the president's signature domestic programs. The deal now faces an unclear outlook in Congress, where progressive Democrats and hardline Republicans have both criticized components of the agreement. •The tentative deal would raise the debt limit on how much the government can borrow through the end of 2024, averting a default that could happen June 5 if the debt ceiling is not raised.•The deal caps annual discretionary spending for two years, keeping non-defense spending levels flat. This means funding for domestic programs across the board − besides Social Security and Medicare − will stay the same next year.• The White House succeeded in keeping intact the Inflation Reduction Act − the president's signature climate and prescription drug bill − and the president's program to forgive student loan debt for millions of Americans, at least for now. Turkey's Erdogan wins another term, could govern until 2029 Turkey's all-powerful leader Recep Tayyip Erdogan won a presidential election runoff Sunday, a victory that means he can extend his rule into a third decade. The vote between Erdogan and challenger Kemal Kilicdaroglu came after neither candidate received at least 50% support from Turkey's voters in a May 14 election. The vote resonates beyond the borders of the NATO ally. Erdogan helped broker a grain deal between warring Russia and Ukraine and blocked Sweden from joining NATO. But he has also eroded Turkey's democratic institutions, aggressively consolidating his own power. Read more President Recep Tayyip Erdogan speaks to supporters at the presidential palace after winning reelection in a runoff on May 29, 2023 in Ankara, Turkey. Chris McGrath, Getty Images More news to know now •A California man stopped a bank robbery by talking to the suspect. It was his former neighbor. •Memorial day weather updates: Thunderstorms in North Carolina, Texas, other states.•Bomb threats were made against Target stores in Utah, Ohio and Pennsylvania over Pride controversy. •Amazon's Memorial Day sale is here—shop 80+ deals on appliances, furniture and more.•On today's 5 Things podcast, the latest after a weekend deal on the debt ceiling. Listen on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or your smart speaker. What's the weather today? Check your local forecast here. Texas House votes to impeach Republican Attorney General Ken Paxton Texas' GOP-led House of Representatives impeached state Attorney General Ken Paxton on Saturday on articles including bribery and abuse of public trust, a sudden, historic rebuke of a fellow Republican who rose to be a star of the conservative legal movement despite years of scandal and alleged crimes. The move constitutes an abrupt downfall for one of the GOP's most prominent legal combatants and triggers Paxton's immediate suspension from office pending the outcome of a trial in the state Senate. Republican Gov. Greg Abbott has remained silent. Read more Rep. Ann Johnson, vice chair of the House General Investigating Committee, holds up a binder of evidence supporting the impeachment of Attorney General Ken Paxton in the House Chamber at the Texas Capitol on Saturday, May 27, 2023, in Austin, Texas. Aaron E. Martinez, AP Kyiv absorbs 'most massive' drone attack of war At least one person has been killed after Kyiv was subjected to the "most massive" drone attack since Russia's invasion of Ukraine began 15 months ago, a five-hour bombardment as the ancient city prepared to celebrate the anniversary of its founding Sunday. Kyiv Mayor Vitali Klitschko said preliminary information indicated Kyiv's air defenses shot down more than 40 drones. Ukraine said that Saturday night was also record-breaking in terms of Shahed drone attacks across the country. The attacks come against the backdrop of Russia's ambassador to the U.K., Andrei Kelin, warning of an escalation in Ukraine. Read more •''The time has come to take back what is ours'': Ukraine signals counteroffensive is coming. •Lindsey Graham's ''Russians are dying'' comments draw strong Kremlin rebuke Just for subscribers: •The horrific accident that took teen athlete's legs changed her life − but not her spirit. •After the war: First-generation Vietnamese Americans are thriving in New Orleans.•They remember: Five decades after America's withdrawal from Vietnam War.• Meet James Cook, whose salute as he exited POW camp in Vietnam was immortalized in photos. These articles are for USA TODAY subscribers. You can sign up here. Josef Newgarden wins first Indy 500 Two-time IndyCar champion Josef Newgarden prevailed in a one-lap shootout with defending race winner Marcus Ericsson on Sunday after incidents in the closing laps brought out multiple red flags in the 107th running of the iconic race at Indianapolis Motor Speedway. Newgarden, who won his 27th career IndyCar race, had never finished better than third in 11 previous attempts at The Brickyard. Read more •Indianapolis 500 fan whose car was hit by flying tire: 'Thought somebody was pranking me'. Josef Newgarden celebrates with milk after winning the Indianapolis 500 at Indianapolis Motor Speedway. Mark J. Rebilas, USA TODAY Sports Quick hits •'Succession' finale': Which backstabbing Roy family member became the Waystar CEO? •The Florida mom who challenged Amanda Gorman's poem and other books says she only read parts of the material.•The Miami Heat are up against the Boston Celtics in Game 7 on Monday. Is Boston on the verge of a comeback?•Blue Ivy Carter performed with her mom Beyoncé at the Renaissance tour. Photo of the day: LSU Tigers celebrate title at White House Nearly two months after winning its first national championship, the LSU women's basketball team took the traditional visit to the White House on Friday – a celebratory afternoon that was briefly interrupted by a scary moment, when an LSU player fainted on the podium. Read more President Joe Biden and first lady Jill Biden are presented with jerseys by LSU women's basketball team captains Angel Reese, right, and Emily Ward, left, during an event to honor the 2023 NCAA national championship team in the East Room of the White House, Friday, May 26, 2023, in Washington. Subject: TikTok sues Montana; what happens if the U.S. defaults; Colorado river states agree on water sharing deal
Body: Plus, hear our best advice for making friends wherever you move. View this email online by Suzanne Nuyen May 23, 2023 Good morning. Peggy Whitson, who broke the record for time spent in space, is back for more than a week at the International Space Station. Here's what else we're following today. Lawmakers have nine days left to agree on a plan for the nation's debt limit and avoid default on its bills, according to Treasury officials. Though Monday's talks ended without a deal, President Biden called them "productive." Alex Brandon/AP 🎧 If the U.S. defaults, social security, taxpayer returns and military benefits could be affected as the government decides which bills to pay first. On Up First today, NPR's Scott Horsley says a default could damage the nation's future borrowing capabilities, similar to how one's credit score falls when bills aren't paid. TikTok is suing Montana after the state passed a law that takes effect next year banning the app. TikTok's lawyers allege the law violates free speech rights and call Montana's concerns over China accessing American data "unfounded speculation.” 🎧 NPR's Bobby Allyn says the First Amendment has historically been a "very strong legal shield," and legal experts tell him the app has a strong case. But if Montana's law is upheld, Allyn reports it could create a "snowball effect," and more states could make copycat laws. Water managers in California, Arizona and Nevada have agreed on a proposal to reduce their water intake from the drought-stricken Colorado River to keep it from running dry in exchange for over a billion dollars in federal payments. The deal runs through the end of 2026. 🎧 Learning to live with less water will likely be "very expensive and potentially very painful" for these states, according to KUNC's Luke Runyon. He adds that the river's story broadens the discussion of adapting to climate change and gives the rest of the country a real-time look at future challenges. Newsletter continues after sponsor message Enlighten me Stan Honda/AFP via Getty Images Enlighten Me is a special series with NPR's Rachel Martin on in-depth conversations about the human condition. How do you move beyond hate and anger after a tragedy? Simran Jeet Singh says his anger after seven people were killed in 2012 in a mass shooting at a Sikh temple was "corrosive." 🎧 In a conversation with NPR's Rachel Martin, he discusses how Sikh philosophy helped him deal with what happened and see the inherent humanity in everyone. Listen to the interview or read it here.One of the biggest challenges of moving is making a new group of friends. Whether you're a college grad moving to a new city or have always been a nomad, we've got strategies for finding a sense of belonging wherever you are. 👋 Tell your loved ones you're moving and see if they can connect you with new people. 👋 Reach out to old friends in new cities. 👋 Find activities and make them routine. With time, you'll see some familiar faces and potential friends. 👋 Don't put too much pressure on yourself to make friends — focus on the connections. Listen to Life Kit's guide to making friends in new places here. Jenn Chávez/OPB Fidele Munyanganizi, a refugee from the Democratic Republic of Congo, created Outgrowing Hunger because he missed the food from his homeland. The project helps refugees grow their cultural crops. (via OPB)A TikToker deep cleaned a couch she found on the street, and it horrified fans. We talked to a bedbug expert about what it takes to truly get rid of the pests.Pittsburgh’s National Aviary’s researchers believe the Ivory-billed woodpecker, thought to be extinct, is still alive but endangered. (via WESA) Listen to your local NPR station. Visit NPR.org to hear live radio from KUNC (edit station). This newsletter was edited by Majd Al-Waheidi. Enjoying this newsletter? Forward to a friend! They can sign up here.Looking for more great content? Check out all of our newsletter offerings — including Music, Politics, Health and more! Subject: Concern about Sudden Stagnation in Data Analytics Dashboard
Body:
Dear Customer Support,
I am contacting you to report a problem with our data analytics dashboard. The dashboard has suddenly stopped working, and we are not able to use any of its functions. This occurred during a recent update to our investment forecasts, and we believe there might be a software incompatibility issue, though we are unsure.
To date, we have attempted to restart the systems and reviewed the logs, but none of these actions have resolved the issue. We would be very grateful if you could investigate this and provide a solution at your earliest convenience.
Please inform us if there is any additional information you require from us to address this problem. We are ready to supply any necessary details or schedule a call to discuss further.
Thank you for promptly addressing this issue. We are looking forward to your prompt response.
